segments.conditions: Segregated coaches were mandatory through Alabama and Tennessee. Birmingham's terminal was a major gathering point for Black Belt migrants leaving the iron and steel district, and the train passed through small-town stations where local sheriffs sometimes tried to block departures during labor shortages.

The route ran north through central Tennessee and into the Kentucky Bluegrass. Nashville itself was a destination for some migrants, but most through-passengers were heading to the Ohio River cities — Louisville, Cincinnati, and beyond to the factories of Cleveland and Detroit.

Crossing the Ohio River carried the same symbolic weight as the Illinois Central's crossing — it meant entering free-state territory. Cincinnati served as a major junction where migrants transferred to lines heading to Cleveland, Detroit, or Chicago, dispersing into the industrial heartland.

who_used_it: Great Migration central corridor from the Alabama Black Belt (Birmingham, Montgomery, Selma) heading north to Louisville, Cincinnati, Cleveland, and Detroit. Also Appalachian out-migration from eastern Kentucky and Tennessee heading to factory jobs in Ohio River cities, particularly during the 1940s-1960s.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} likely rode the Louisville and Nashville Railroad from {origin} toward {destination}, part of the central migration corridor that carried tens of thousands of Black Alabamians and Appalachian families north to the Ohio River factory cities.
source_references: Maury Klein, 'History of the Louisville & Nashville Railroad' (Macmillan, 1972)
Isabel Wilkerson, 'The Warmth of Other Suns: The Epic Story of America's Great Migration' (Random House, 2010)
Robert J. Norrell, 'Up from History: The Life of Booker T. Washington' (Harvard University Press, 2009)
Chad Berry, 'Southern Migrants, Northern Exiles' (University of Illinois Press, 2000)
